2026-03-25T21:41:50.0716873Z Agent: 
2026-03-25T21:41:50.0719785Z Agent: The correct approach is to navigate into the dev-tool directory and run `npm install -g` from there, or just use `npx dev-tool` directly as shown in the next line. The README confirms this: you either run `npx dev-tool` from a service package, or `cd common/tools/dev-tool && npm install -g` to install globally—not `npm install -g common/tools/dev-tool`.
2026-03-25T21:41:50.0722141Z Agent: 
2026-03-25T21:41:50.0724119Z Agent: Wait, actually `npm install -g <path>` does install from that local path globally, which is equivalent to changing into the directory first. But I'm noticing the instructions use `npm` when this is a `pnpm` workspace, and since the next command just uses `npx dev-tool`, the global installation might not even be necessary.
2026-03-25T21:41:50.0726261Z Agent: 
2026-03-25T21:41:50.0727722Z Agent: Looking at the dev-tool README, it says you can run `npx dev-tool` from a service package as long as `@azure/dev-tool` is in devDependencies. So the global install step seems redundant, though the documentation specifies it anyway.

The trailing `...` is crucial because it tells pnpm to build not just the specified package but all its dependencies too.... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2640 chars]

2026-03-25T21:41:50.0819042Z Agent: 
2026-03-25T21:41:50.0820449Z Agent: However, looking at the example more closely, it's showing a dependency tree from an older version of the SDK that still used `@azure/core-http`. While the package is deprecated, it still exists on npm, and the document is primarily teaching the concept of managing transitive dependencies rather than providing a current-day example. The historical context might actually be fine to leave as-is for educational purposes.

2026-03-25T21:41:50.0865930Z Agent: 
2026-03-25T21:41:50.0868120Z Agent: Looking at the global install approach more closely, the current instructions use `npm` to install globally from the repo root, then run `npx dev-tool` from the project directory. But since `dev-tool` is already a local dependency, the global install step is redundant—I can simplify this to just run `npx dev-tool` directly, which will find the package in the local node_modules without needing the global installation or the inconsistent npm command. Now I'm going to apply these fixes to the documentation files.

2026-03-25T21:41:50.2865154Z {"timestamp":"2026-03-25T21:40:54.741406618Z","direction":"OUT","type":"REQUEST","server_id":"safeoutputs","method":"tools/call","payload":{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"tools/call","params":{"arguments":{"body":"## Summary\n\nThis PR fixes six documentation inconsistencies found across five files in the `documentation/` folder after reviewing every markdown file against the current source code and tooling.\n\n---\n\n## Changes\n\n### `documentation/Generate-code-from-TypeSpec.md`\n**Issue:** Incorrect pnpm global install command: `npm install -g @pnpm`  \n**Fix:** Changed to `npm install -g pnpm`  \n**Why:** The pnpm package on npm is named `pnpm`, not `@pnpm`. The `@` prefix denotes a scoped package (e.g. `@azure/...`) and is incorrect here — `npm install -g @pnpm` would fail or install a different/wrong package.\n\n---\n\n### `documentation/golden-testing-commands.md`\n**Issue (×2):** Two code examples referenced outdated test script names that no longer exist in modern SDK packages:\n- `npm run unit-test:node` (Method 1 section)\n- `npm run integration-test:node` (Method 2 section)\n\n**Fix:** Both updated to `npm run test:node`  \n**Why:** Current Azure SDK packages use `test:node` as the test script name (backed by `dev-tool run test:vitest`). The old `unit-test:node` / `integration-test:node` scripts no longer exist and would produce an error if run.\n\n---\n\n### `documentation/steps-after-generations.md`\n**Issue:** Incorrect global install instruction before using `npx dev-tool`:\n```shell\nnpm install -g common/tools/dev-tool # make sure you are in the azure-sdk-for-js repo root directory\n```\n**Fix:** Removed the `npm install -g` line entirely; the `cd ${PROJECT_ROOT} \u0026\u0026 npx dev-tool samples publish -f` that follows is sufficient.  \n**Why:** In a pnpm workspace, `npx dev-tool` resolves the `@azure/dev-tool` binary from the workspace's `node_modules` without requiring a global install. The global install step used `npm` (not `pnpm`) in an otherwise pnpm-managed project, and it was entirely redundant.\n\n---\n\n### `documentation/next-generation-quickstart.md`\n**Issue:** Missing `@` scope prefix on a package name: `` `azure/arm-resources` ``  \n**Fix:** Changed to `` `@azure/arm-resources` ``  \n**Why:** All Azure SDK packages are scoped under `@azure/`. Every other package in the same sentence already had the correct `@azure/` prefix; this was a typo on `arm-resources` alone.\n\n---\n\n### `documentation/resolve-pnpm-lock-merge-conflict.md`\n**Issue:** Missing path separator `/` in the one-liner shell command:\n```shell\ngit checkout upstream/main `git rev-parse --show-toplevel`/pnpm-lock.yaml \u0026\u0026 pnpm install \u0026\u0026 git add `git rev-parse --show-toplevel`pnpm-lock.yaml\n```\nThe `git add` part concatenates the repo root path directly with `pnpm-lock.yaml` (no separator), producing an invalid path like `/path/to/repopnpm-lock.yaml`.\n\n**Fix:** Added the missing `/`:\n```shell\ngit checkout upstream/main `git rev-parse --show-toplevel`/pnpm-lock.yaml \u0026\u0026 pnpm install \u0026\u0026 git add `git rev-parse --show-toplevel`/pnpm-lock.yaml\n```\n**Why:** The `git checkout` part of the same command already had the `/` separator; the `git add` part was missing it, causing `git add` to silently fail (no file would be staged).\n\n---\n\n## Testing\nDocumentation-only changes; no build or test execution required.\n","branch":"docs/fix-documentation-inconsistencies","draft":true,"integrity":"high","secrecy":"public","title":"fix: documentation inconsistencies in documentation/ folder"},"name":"create_pull_request"}}}

2026-03-25T21:41:50.3161482Z ---
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3161491Z 
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3161563Z ## Changes
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3161567Z 
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3161696Z ### `documentation/Generate-code-from-TypeSpec.md`
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3161884Z **Issue:** Incorrect pnpm global install command: `npm install -g @pnpm`  
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3161983Z **Fix:** Changed to `npm install -g pnpm`  
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3162734Z **Why:** The pnpm package on npm is named `pnpm`, not `@pnpm`. The `@` prefix denotes a scoped package (e.g. `@azure/...`) and is incorrect here — `npm install -g @pnpm` would fail or install a different/wrong package.
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3162745Z 
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3162807Z ---
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3162811Z 
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3162931Z ### `documentation/golden-testing-commands.md`
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3163328Z **Issue (×2):** Two code examples referenced outdated test script names that no longer exist in modern SDK packages:
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3163432Z - `npm run unit-test:node` (Method 1 section)
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3163566Z - `npm run integration-test:node` (Method 2 section)
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3163570Z 
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3163667Z **Fix:** Both updated to `npm run test:node`  
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3164158Z **Why:** Current Azure SDK packages use `test:node` as the test script name (backed by `dev-tool run test:vitest`). The old `unit-test:node` / `integration-test:node` scripts no
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3164221Z ...
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3164345Z (truncated, showing first 65536 bytes of 74921 total)
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3164581Z ##[endgroup]
2026-03-25T21:41:50.3164794Z ##[group]tools.json (5356 bytes)
